# Simple Session and Repeating Timer

*Generated by Forge on 2026-04-14T12:37:39.405Z — score 8/10*

## Overview
Simple Session and Repeating Timer is a mobile-first web app for people who need a timer that is visible from across a room and structured around real sessions, not generic one-off countdowns. The problem is that default phone timers are built for personal close-range use, while most timer apps are either cluttered HIIT tools, Pomodoro apps, or basic countdown utilities that do not support named segments, repeating routines, spoken alerts, and audience-facing readability. This product solves that by offering a clean, fast timer experience with two core modes—session timer and repeating interval timer—plus reusable presets, large display mode, and spoken transitions for teaching, presentations, workouts, drills, laundry, and other recurring routines.

## Target user
Primary persona: a teacher, coach, presenter, trainer, or facilitator who runs structured activities in front of other people and uses their phone, tablet, or laptop as a visible timer.

What they do now:
- Use the default phone timer or stopwatch
- Use a fitness interval timer that feels overbuilt and badly branded for non-fitness contexts
- Use a presentation timer that does not support repeating routines
- Recreate the same timer sequence manually every day

Why current options fail them:
- Text is too small to read from across the room
- No support for named segments like “Warmup”, “Group Work”, “Q&A”, or “Rest”
- Repeating routines are awkward or impossible to set up
- Existing apps are cluttered, ad-heavy, or strongly fitness-branded
- Transition alerts are not configurable or spoken clearly
- Saving and reusing simple presets is harder than it should be

The ideal user is not looking for “another timer app.” They want a purpose-built visible session timer for structured real-world routines.

## Core features
- Email/Google sign-in and guest mode
  - Let users try the app without creating an account
  - Require account only to save presets across devices
- Two timer creation modes
  - Session mode: one sequence of named segments run once
  - Repeating mode: one or more named segments repeated for N cycles or until manually stopped
- Segment builder
  - Add/edit/delete/reorder segments
  - Each segment has name, duration, optional color
- Preset saving
  - Save a timer setup as a named preset
  - Duplicate, rename, delete presets
- Large display timer screen
  - Full-screen readable countdown
  - Current segment name shown in large text
  - Next segment preview shown smaller
  - Progress bar or color background for quick visual recognition
- Timer controls
  - Start, pause, resume, skip segment, previous segment, restart session
- Spoken and sound alerts
  - Play short sound at segment transitions
  - Optional text-to-speech announcement of segment name
- Simple settings
  - Toggle sound alerts on/off
  - Toggle spoken alerts on/off
  - Choose voice language from browser-supported voices if available
- Preset templates
  - Preloaded starter presets: Presentation, Classroom Rotation, Workout Intervals, Laundry, Study Session
- Responsive mobile-first UI
  - Optimized for phone first, but usable on tablet and desktop
- Stripe paywall
  - Free tier with limited saved presets
  - Pro unlock for unlimited presets and advanced display options
- Basic analytics/events
  - Track preset created, timer started, timer completed, upgrade clicked, subscription activated

## Tech stack recommendation
Because App Store distribution is not required for the MVP and the core product can work well as a mobile-first installable web app, use the standard web stack. This will ship faster, avoid App Store review overhead, and still serve the target use case on phones, tablets, and laptops through a responsive PWA-style experience. The app does not require native-only device APIs beyond browser audio and speech synthesis.

Recommended stack:
- Next.js 15.x (App Router) + TypeScript 5.x
- PostgreSQL 16 + Prisma ORM 5.x
- Tailwind CSS 3.x + shadcn/ui
- NextAuth v5 with Google OAuth + email magic link fallback if desired later
- Stripe for subscriptions
- Resend for transactional emails
- Deployed to Coolify using Docker
- PWA support via `next-pwa` or a lightweight manual manifest/service worker setup
- Zod for schema validation
- React Hook Form for form handling
- Zustand for local timer/session state
- Framer Motion for small UI transitions
- date-fns for duration formatting
- `use-sound` or native HTMLAudioElement for alert sounds
- Browser `speechSynthesis` API for spoken alerts
- PostHog or Plausible for analytics; choose Plausible if keeping it simple and privacy-friendly

Specific recommendations:
- `next@15`
- `react@19`
- `typescript@5`
- `prisma@5`
- `@prisma/client@5`
- `next-auth@5` or `@auth/core` / `@auth/prisma-adapter`
- `stripe@17`
- `resend@4`
- `zod@3`
- `react-hook-form@7`
- `zustand@5`
- `framer-motion@11`

Why this stack:
- Fastest path for a solo developer
- Works well for SEO landing pages plus authenticated app
- Supports subscriptions cleanly
- Can be installed to home screen as a web app
- Avoids native app complexity until demand is proven

## Monetization
Primary strategy: freemium with one paid tier.

Why:
- Timer utilities are easy to try and easy to abandon, so free access is necessary for adoption
- The value becomes obvious after repeated use, making upgrade prompts around presets and display modes more natural
- One simple Pro tier keeps the product understandable and maintainable

Pricing:
- Free
  - Up to 3 saved presets
  - Unlimited manual timer runs
  - Basic large display mode
  - Sound alerts
  - 1 custom repeating timer
- Pro: $3.99/month or $24.99/year
  - Unlimited saved presets
  - Unlimited repeating timers
  - Spoken alerts
  - Advanced display themes/colors
  - Duplicate/edit template library
  - Priority support
  - Future access to export/share presets

Recommendation:
- Default upsell should push annual plan at $24.99/year
- Monthly exists for lower-friction conversion
- No lifetime plan in MVP; it complicates pricing and reduces recurring revenue

## Keyword strategy
This is a web app, so prioritize SEO landing pages plus app-focused conversion pages.

Primary SEO keywords:

## Revenue model
Pricing strategy:
- Free tier to maximize acquisition and search conversion
- Pro monthly: $3.99
- Pro annual: $24.99
- Assume 70% of paid users choose annual, 30% choose monthly after month 3
- Effective blended monthly ARPU from paid users: roughly $2.36 in early months, growing toward $2.75 as annual adoption improves

Assumptions:
- Strong organic acquisition from long-tail SEO and direct utility intent
- Some paid acquisition from Apple Search Ads equivalent is not relevant here, so use Google Search + Meta/short-form video tests for web
- Free-to-paid conversion: 2% month 1, 3% month 3, 3.5% month 6, 4% month 12
- Free user retention driven by repeated routine use

Projected users and revenue:

Month 1
- 800 total signups
- 784 free users
- 16 paid users at 2% conversion
- Blended ARPU paid: $2.25
- MRR: about $36
- Realistic total collected cash if some annual plans convert upfront: $150-$250

Month 3
- 3,000 total cumulative signups
- 2,910 free users
- 90 paid users at 3% conversion
- Blended ARPU paid: $2.40
- MRR: about $216
- Cash collected in month: $400-$900 depending on annual mix

Month 6
- 8,000 total cumulative signups
- 7,720 free users
- 280 paid users at 3.5% conversion
- Blended ARPU paid: $2.55
- MRR: about $714
- Cash collected in month: $1,000-$2,000

Month 12
- 20,000 total cumulative signups
- 19,200 free users
- 800 paid users at 4% conversion
- Blended ARPU paid: $2.75
- MRR: about $2,200
- Cash collected in month: $2,500-$5,000

Conservative scenario from provided market data:
- Mature monthly revenue: $1,500-$4,000
- This likely requires 500-1,400 active paid subscribers or strong annual-plan cash flow

Optimistic scenario:
- $8,000-$20,000/month
- Requires strong SEO rankings, viral template sharing, and clear dominance in sub-niches like classroom timer and presentation timer

Funnel:
- Landing page visitor → starts free timer without signup
- Prompt to save preset → signup
- Uses preset multiple times → upgrade prompt when hitting preset limit or wanting spoken alerts
- Upgrade → annual plan emphasized

## Competitor landscape
### Seconds Interval Timer
- Pricing model: freemium/subscription, varies by platform
- Key features:
  - Advanced interval programming
  - Audio cues
  - Workout-oriented workflows
  - Multiple training formats
- Gaps/weaknesses:
  - Strongly associated with HIIT/fitness
  - Overbuilt for teachers, speakers, and general routines
  - UI can feel dense for users who just want named visible segments
- Our differentiation:
  - Neutral branding
  - Simpler setup
  - Better across-the-room readability
  - Better fit for classrooms, presentations, drills, and routines

### Time Timer
- Pricing model: paid app / premium pricing, often around $9.99+
- Key features:
  - Strong visual countdown metaphor
  - Good for glanceable elapsed/remaining time
  - Recognizable brand in education/ADHD contexts
- Gaps/weaknesses:
  - More focused on visual countdown than structured multi-step routines
  - Weak repeating interval support
  - No strong named-segment workflow
  - App experience reported as clunky/expensive by some users
- Our differentiation:
  - Named segments
  - Repeating routines
  - Spoken transitions
  - Lower entry price and free tier

### Stagestime
- Pricing model: subscription-gated presentation timer
- Key features:
  - Presentation-focused timing
  - Stage/speaking support
  - Segment logic for talks
- Gaps/weaknesses:
  - More complex setup
  - Narrow presentation framing
  - Does not cover repeating interval use cases well
- Our differentiation:
  - Unified app for both speaking and repeating routines
  - Faster setup for everyday use
  - Broader utility across teachers, coaches, and workouts

### Tabata Stopwatch Pro / similar Tabata timers
- Pricing model: freemium or low-cost paid
- Key features:
  - Workout interval timing
  - Audio cues
  - Tabata protocol support
- Gaps/weaknesses:
  - Locked to workout framing
  - Limited custom naming
  - Not useful for presentations or classroom blocks
  - Often visually cluttered
- Our differentiation:
  - Generic interval engine
  - Named segments for any routine
  - Non-fitness positioning
  - Better visible display mode

### Multi Timer
- Pricing model: freemium/premium
- Key features:
  - Multiple simultaneous timers
  - Utility-focused flexibility
- Gaps/weaknesses:
  - Cluttered UI
  - Not audience-facing
  - Not optimized for one structured session
  - Repeating presets are not the main workflow
- Our differentiation:
  - Opinionated single-session flow
  - Faster to configure
  - Better readability
  - Better preset reuse for recurring routines

## Timeline
### Week 1-2: core MVP
Week 1
- Initialize Next.js app, Prisma, auth, Stripe scaffolding
- Build landing page and app shell
- Create database models for users, presets, segments, subscriptions
- Implement guest mode with local storage
- Build timer creation UI for Session mode
- Build segment CRUD: add, edit, delete, reorder
- Build timer runner with start/pause/resume/restart

Week 2
- Add Repeating mode with cycle count
- Build full-screen display mode
- Add current segment and next segment preview
- Add sound alerts at transitions
- Add spoken alerts using browser speech synthesis
- Add preset save/load for signed-in users
- Add starter templates

### Week 3: polish + testing
- Add free tier limits and upgrade prompts
- Integrate Stripe checkout and customer portal
- Implement subscription gating middleware/helpers
- Improve responsive layout for phone/tablet/desktop
- Add settings page for sound, speech, theme
- Add analytics events
- Test timer accuracy, background tab behavior, and mobile browser edge cases

### Week 4: beta + marketing prep
- Invite 20-30 beta users from teacher/coaching/speaking communities
- Fix onboarding friction and timer builder pain points
- Create 5 SEO landing pages and 5 template pages
- Record demo videos and screenshots
- Write launch posts and outreach messages
- Set up email capture and waitlist for homepage visitors if needed

### Week 5: launch + distribution
- Launch on Product Hunt if desired, but not as primary channel
- Announce in target communities with use-case-specific posts
- Start paid search tests
- Publish blog content and short demo videos
- Monitor conversion funnel and improve paywall copy
- Ship first post-launch improvements based on beta feedback

## Score breakdown
- recurringRevenue: 6/10 — recurring use supports subscriptions, but utility apps have modest ARPU and many substitutes.
- buildComplexity: 9/10 — the MVP is technically straightforward with standard web tech and limited backend complexity.
- marketValidation: 8/10 — clear pain points show up across teachers, speakers, coaches, and productivity users.
- competition: 4/10 — timer apps are crowded, so success depends on niche positioning rather than broad utility competition.
- automationPotential: 9/10 — onboarding, billing, preset reuse, and self-serve growth can be highly automated.
- platformFit: 9/10 — a mobile-first web app is a strong fit for this use case and can ship quickly without native overhead.

## Build instructions for Codex
1. Initialize project: `npx create-next-app@latest simple-session-repeating-timer --typescript --tailwind --eslint --app --src-dir --import-alias "@/*"`.

2. Install core dependencies: `npm install prisma @prisma/client next-auth @auth/prisma-adapter stripe resend zod react-hook-form zustand date-fns framer-motion` and dev dependencies needed for Prisma and types.

3. Install shadcn/ui: `npx shadcn@latest init` and add components: `button`, `card`, `dialog`, `dropdown-menu`, `form`, `input`, `label`, `select`, `sheet`, `switch`, `tabs`, `textarea`, `toast`, `tooltip`, `badge`, `separator`.

4. Set up Prisma with `npx prisma init`. Configure PostgreSQL connection in `.env`. Define schema models in `prisma/schema.prisma`:
   - `User`
   - `Account`
   - `Session`
   - `VerificationToken`
   - `Preset`
   - `Segment`
   - `UserSettings`
   - `Subscription`
   Suggested shapes:
   - `Preset`: `id`, `userId`, `name`, `mode` (`SESSION` or `REPEATING`), `repeatCount`, `isTemplate`, `createdAt`, `updatedAt`
   - `Segment`: `id`, `presetId`, `name`, `durationSeconds`, `color`, `position`
   - `UserSettings`: `userId`, `soundEnabled`, `speechEnabled`, `voiceName`, `theme`
   - `Subscription`: `userId`, `stripeCustomerId`, `stripeSubscriptionId`, `stripePriceId`, `status`, `currentPeriodEnd`
   Run `npx prisma generate` and `npx prisma migrate dev`.

5. Configure NextAuth in `src/auth.ts` and `src/app/api/auth/[...nextauth]/route.ts` using Google OAuth and Prisma adapter. Add sign-in and sign-out actions. Include a guest mode path that stores presets in localStorage when unauthenticated.

6. Create app route structure:
   - `src/app/page.tsx` for homepage
   - `src/app/(marketing)/presentation-timer/page.tsx`
   - `src/app/(marketing)/visual-timer/page.tsx`
   - `src/app/(marketing)/interval-timer/page.tsx`
   - `src/app/(marketing)/repeating-timer/page.tsx`
   - `src/app/app/page.tsx` for main dashboard
   - `src/app/app/presets/page.tsx`
   - `src/app/app/settings/page.tsx`
   - `src/app/app/timer/[presetId]/page.tsx`
   - `src/app/pricing/page.tsx`

7. Build homepage and marketing pages with clear positioning: visible timer for teachers, speakers, coaches, and routines. Add CTA buttons for “Start Free Timer” and “Browse Templates.” Include structured metadata in each page via Next.js metadata exports.

8. Create TypeScript domain types in `src/types/timer.ts`:
   - `TimerMode`
   - `SegmentInput`
   - `PresetInput`
   - `RunningSessionState`
   Include validation schemas in `src/lib/validators/timer.ts` using Zod.

9. Build timer builder components:
   - `src/components/timer/timer-builder.tsx`
   - `src/components/timer/segment-list.tsx`
   - `src/components/timer/segment-form.tsx`
   - `src/components/timer/mode-toggle.tsx`
   - `src/components/timer/repeat-count-input.tsx`
   Use React Hook Form + Zod. Support add/edit/delete/reorder segments and mode switching between session and repeating.

10. Implement local timer engine in `src/lib/timer-engine.ts`. It should:
   - Accept ordered segments and repeat count
   - Track current segment index, cycle number, remaining seconds, running/paused/completed state
   - Expose methods: `start`, `pause`, `resume`, `skipNext`, `goPrevious`, `restart`
   - Handle transition logic cleanly for both session and repeating modes

11. Create Zustand store in `src/stores/timer-store.ts` for active timer state. Persist only builder drafts locally, not running timer state across sessions in MVP. Use `setInterval` with timestamp drift correction so countdown stays accurate.

12. Build timer runner UI:
   - `src/components/timer/timer-display.tsx`
   - `src/components/timer/fullscreen-timer.tsx`
   - `src/components/timer/timer-controls.tsx`
   Show large countdown, current segment name, next segment preview, cycle count, progress bar, and color theme. Add a full-screen mode button using the browser Fullscreen API.

13. Implement alerts:
   - `src/lib/audio.ts` for sound cues
   - `src/lib/speech.ts` for browser `speechSynthesis`
   On segment transition, play sound if enabled and announce segment name if speech is enabled. Add graceful fallback if speech synthesis is unavailable.

14. Build preset persistence:
   - Server actions or route handlers in `src/app/app/presets/actions.ts`
   - CRUD operations for presets and segments
   - For guests, mirror the same shape in localStorage under a key like `ssrt_guest_presets`
   Add logic to prompt guest users to sign in when trying to save more than the free local limit.

15. Add starter templates seeded in code or DB:
   - Presentation: Intro / Main Talk / Q&A
   - Classroom Rotation: Station 1 / Station 2 / Station 3
   - Workout Intervals: Work / Rest repeated
   - Laundry: Wash / Dry / Fold
   - Study Session: Focus / Break repeated
   Store template definitions in `src/lib/templates.ts`.

16. Set up Stripe:
   - Create one product: `Pro`
   - Create monthly and annual prices
   - Add checkout button on pricing page
   - Implement webhook route at `src/app/api/webhooks/stripe/route.ts`
   - Sync customer and subscription status into `Subscription` table
   - Add billing portal link
   Create helper `src/lib/billing.ts` with `isPro(user)` and free-tier enforcement logic.

17. Enforce free tier limits:
   - Max 3 saved presets for free users
   - Spoken alerts and unlimited repeating presets gated to Pro
   - Show upgrade modal component `src/components/billing/upgrade-modal.tsx` when limits are hit
   - Add pricing page comparison table

18. Build settings page:
   - `src/app/app/settings/page.tsx`
   - Controls for sound enabled, speech enabled, preferred voice, theme
   - Save to `UserSettings` for authenticated users and localStorage for guests
   Include a “Test alert” button.

19. Add analytics and instrumentation:
   - Track events: `timer_started`, `timer_completed`, `preset_created`, `template_used`, `upgrade_clicked`, `checkout_started`, `subscription_activated`
   - Put helper in `src/lib/analytics.ts`
   - Add basic funnel tracking on homepage CTA and pricing page

20. Finalize production readiness:
   - Add sitemap and robots files
   - Add metadata/Open Graph images
   - Create `Dockerfile` with standalone output
   - Set `next.config.ts` for `output: "standalone"`
   - Run `npm run build` and fix all type/lint issues
   - Deploy PostgreSQL and app via Coolify
   - Configure environment variables for database, auth, Stripe, Resend, and app URL
   - Smoke test guest flow, auth flow, Stripe checkout, preset saving, timer accuracy, and full-screen display on mobile and desktop
